title: Terahertz magneto-nanoscopy of encapsulated monolayer graphene
authors: Richard H. J. Kim, Sunwoong Yang, Taehoon Kim, Samuel J. Haeuser, Joong-Mok Park, Randall K. Chan, Thomas Koschny, Young-Mi Bahk, Sung Ju Hong, Jigang Wang
abstract: This study investigates the nanoscale conductivity of encapsulated monolayer graphene at temperatures down to 5 K and magnetic fields of up to 1 T. We use the scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) technique to probe magnetic-field-dependent responses from graphene close to charge neutrality in the terahertz spectral region. We observe the near-perfect high-$q$ reflector behavior of graphene but with subtle changes by the presence of magnetic fields. Measurements align with calculations of the magneto-optical conductivity and the near-field spectroscopic contrast that describes the field-tunable cyclotron resonance of Dirac fermions. Our result provides an initial step toward understanding temperature and magnetic-field effects on nanoscale terahertz transport in two-dimensional quantum materials.
summary: This paper uses THz near-field microscopy to probe the local conductivity of encapsulated graphene at very low temperatures and in magnetic fields. It shows that graphene behaves as a near-perfect reflector for THz waves, but this reflectivity is subtly tuned by magnetic fields. The results align with theoretical predictions of cyclotron resonance in Dirac fermions. This work provides a foundation for studying nanoscale THz transport in 2D quantum materials.
main_problem: Investigating the nanoscale terahertz conductivity and magnetotransport properties of encapsulated monolayer graphene at cryogenic temperatures and under magnetic fields.
main_result: The study demonstrates that graphene acts as a near-perfect high-q reflector in the THz regime, with measurable changes in near-field amplitude observed under magnetic fields up to 1 T, consistent with cyclotron resonance of Dirac fermions.
method: Scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) using a cantilever-based AFM, combined with time-domain THz spectroscopy and magneto-optical conductivity calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is monolayer graphene encapsulated between two flakes of hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN/graphene/h-BN) on a SiO2/Si substrate, studied in a split-pair magnet cryostat.
key_observables: Near-field amplitude (S2), topography, THz near-field images, relative amplitude spectra, and longitudinal magneto-optical conductivity.
important_parameters: Temperatures down to 5 K, magnetic fields up to 1 T, THz frequency range of 0.5 to 1.8 THz, and tip radius of 40 nm.
main_assumptions: Assumed charge-neutral graphene, omission of h-BN layers in the transfer matrix model for simplicity, and use of a phenomenological width for Landau level transitions.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and THz near-field maps; Fig 2 displays line scans across edges and signal variations at different magnetic fields; Fig 3 presents THz time traces, Fourier-transformed spectra, and relative amplitude spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and sample preparation, presents THz near-field imaging and topography, details the magneto-optical response and conductivity measurements, compares experimental data to theoretical models, and concludes with the implications for 2D quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Terahertz nanoimaging of encapsulated monolayer graphene. (a) Schematic of the monolayer graphene sandwiched by h-BN flakes and the terahertz near-field measurement scheme based on an atomic force microscope. (b) A 20 µm by 20 µm terahertz near-field image of graphene at room temperature. The borders of top and bottom h- BN and gold electrode are respectively outlined by blue, purple, and white dashed lines for clarity. Low temperature (c) topography and (d) terahertz near-field mapping of graphene in the red boxed region shown in (b) at zero magnetic field. Graphene appears bright in (b) and (d).

FIG. 2. Magnetic-field-dependent terahertz near-field contrast of graphene. Terahertz near-field amplitude line scans across (a) a gold microstructure edge and (b) the edge of graphene at 5 K and 1 T. Gold and graphene lies on the left half in each of the line scans. (c) Average and standard deviations of near-field signals from graphene line scans at different magnetic fields from 0 to 1 T. The scattered signals S2 are normalized to the average value obtained from gold each taken at the corresponding field value.

FIG. 4. Magneto-optical conductivity and measured terahertz scat- tering near-field amplitude of graphene. Calculated (a) real-part and (b) imaginary part of the longitudinal conductivity of graphene at different terahertz frequencies as a function of the magnetic field. (c) Calculated S2 amplitude of graphene with respect to gold as a function of magnetic field using the conductivity values from (a) and (b). Experimental values are identical to those shown in Fig. 2(c). (d) Calculated S2 amplitude of graphene with respect to gold as a function of frequency for different magnetic field values and the ex- perimental values taken from Fig. 3(c).

FIG. 3. Terahertz magneto-nanospectroscopy of graphene. (a) Ter- ahertz near-field time traces measured on gold and graphene at 5 K and 1 T. (b) Fourier-transformed spectra of the time traces in (a). (c) Relative amplitude spectra of graphene with respect to the spectra taken from gold at fields of 0.1, 0.2, and 1 T. Standard deviations were calculated from data points showing the noise level at the be- ginning of the time-domain traces.

FIG. 5. Calculated S2 of graphene as a function of magnetic field and frequency in the terahertz range using Eq. (1) together with the finite dipole model and the transfer matrix model. The dashed box repre- sents the space surveyed in this study, and circled markers correspond to the cyclotron resonance peaks at 3, 5, and 10 THz as presented in Fig. 4(c).
